seoTitle: How to Create a Reusable Email Template in Brevo
metaDescription: Design a reusable Brevo email template with the visual editor, saving fonts, buttons, and branding once so you don't rebuild layouts every campaign.
intro: Starting every newsletter from scratch each week wastes time and leads to inconsistent branding. Brevo's template management console lets you build a reusable layout once, with your fonts, buttons, and branding locked in, so future campaigns start from a consistent foundation instead of a blank page.

steps.heading: Launch a blank template canvas
steps.body: Open the template management console and start a new blank canvas to design your reusable layout.

steps.heading: Build responsive structural zones
steps.body: Drag in fluid, responsive multi-column zones that will hold your content across future campaigns.

steps.heading: Embed persistent header branding
steps.body: Add your logo and header branding elements that should remain consistent across every email built from this template.

steps.heading: Set global fonts and buttons
steps.body: Configure font choices and button styling once so they apply consistently every time the template is reused.

steps.heading: Inject dynamic variables and save
steps.body: Add dynamic contact variables where needed, then lock down and save the file layout for use in future campaigns.

faq.question: Can I edit a saved template later?
faq.answer: Yes, saved templates can be reopened and adjusted in the template management console at any time.

faq.question: Does a template guarantee consistent branding across campaigns?
faq.answer: Yes, since fonts, buttons, and header branding are locked into the template, reusing it keeps your visual identity consistent.

faq.question: Can I use dynamic contact variables inside a template?
faq.answer: Yes, you can inject dynamic contact variables into a template just as you would in a standalone campaign.

faq.question: How is a template different from a regular campaign draft?
faq.answer: A template is meant to be reused as a starting structure for future campaigns, while a draft is typically a one-off, campaign-specific version.
